did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper explicitly uses machine learning and deep learning methods for the classification of dermatoscopic images of skin neoplasms. Specifically, it employs multi-class and binary cascade two-stage classification algorithms based on neural network architectures and visual transformers (e.g., vit-large-patch32-384, swinv2-tiny-patch4-window16-256, convnext-base-224, and SeyedAli/Melanoma-Classification). The study reports experimental results including Accuracy and F-measure metrics for detecting malignant skin neoplasms using these AI models on datasets including ISIC-2019 and a custom dataset of 657 images.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper is explicitly labeled as 'Оригинальные исследования' (Original Research) in the Russian text and presents original experimental results. It details the development of a screening method using machine learning (visual transformers and neural networks) on specific datasets (ISIC-2019 and a custom dataset of 657 images), reports quantitative performance metrics (Accuracy and F-measure), and proposes a new clinical application based on these new findings.

title: Screening examination method for early differential diagnosis of skin neoplasms using mobile dermatoscopy
brief_summary: This study developed a screening method for the early differential diagnosis of malignant skin neoplasms using mobile dermatoscopy combined with machine learning algorithms. The researchers employed multi-class and binary cascade two-stage classification models based on neural network and visual transformer architectures, such as vit-large-patch32-384 and SeyedAli/Melanoma-Classification. The models were trained and evaluated on a dataset comprising the ISIC-2019 collection and a custom set of 657 clinically verified images. Experimental results showed that the best multi-class classification model achieved an Accuracy of 0.932 and an F-measure of 0.891 for eight types of neoplasms. Additionally, the binary cascade classification achieved high performance, with an Accuracy of 0.964 and F-measure of 0.951 for distinguishing melanomas from nevi in the second stage. The authors conclude that this methodology can effectively support physician decision-making in mass preventive examinations and primary care settings.

all_task_types.target: Eight types of skin neoplasms (melanoma, nevus, basal cell carcinoma, actinic keratosis, seborrheic keratosis, dermatofibroma, vascular lesions, squamous cell carcinoma)
all_task_types.details: Multi-class classification of dermatoscopic images into 8 nosological groups using neural network and visual transformer architectures (e.g., SeyedAli/Melanoma-Classification, vit-large-patch32-384). Evaluated on ISIC-2019 and a custom dataset of 657 images. Best model achieved Accuracy 0.932 and F-measure 0.891.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification: Melanocytic vs. Non-melanocytic neoplasms (Stage 1) and Melanoma vs. Nevus (Stage 2)
all_task_types.details: Binary cascade two-stage classification. Stage 1 distinguished melanocytic from non-melanocytic neoplasms (Accuracy 0.954, F-measure 0.948). Stage 2 differentiated melanoma from nevus within the melanocytic class (Accuracy 0.964, F-measure 0.951).

datasets_used.name: ISIC-2019
datasets_used.description: A public dataset of dermatoscopic images used for training and testing classification models. The study utilized 24,765 images from this collection.

use.training: Used as part of the combined dataset for training multi-class and binary cascade classification models.
use.testing: Used as part of the combined dataset for testing multi-class and binary cascade classification models.

datasets_used.name: Custom Dataset (Authors' Collection)
datasets_used.description: A private dataset of clinically verified dermatoscopic images collected by the authors, specifically including cases characteristic of the Russian population (Fitzpatrick skin types I–IV).

use.training: Used as part of the combined dataset for training multi-class and binary cascade classification models.
use.testing: Used as part of the combined dataset for testing multi-class and binary cascade classification models.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using Accuracy and F-measure metrics on specific datasets (ISIC-2019 and a custom dataset) without statistical significance testing.

multi_class_classification.notes: Best model was SeyedAli/Melanoma-Classification with 100 epochs.

binary_cascade_stage_1_melanocytic_vs_no.notes: Evaluated on ISIC-2019 dataset. Best model was microsoft/swinv2-tiny-patch4-window16-256 with batch size 16 and 100 epochs.

binary_cascade_stage_2_melanoma_vs_nevus.notes: Evaluated on a subset of melanocytic images from the first stage. Best model was SeyedAli/Melanoma-Classification with batch size 16 and 100 epochs.

architecture_used.main_model: Multiple models from Hugging Face repository were evaluated, including Vision Transformer (ViT) and Swin Transformer architectures. Specific models used include: SeyedAli/Melanoma-Classification (ViT-based), microsoft/swinv2-tiny-patch4-window16-256, UnipaPolitoUnimore/vit-large-patch32-384-melanoma, Oppoizer/Output-prova_melanoma, Heem2/Melanoma-Cancer-Image-classification, and others.
architecture_used.pretraining: Models were sourced from open repositories (Hugging Face) and fine-tuned on the authors' dataset. Specific pretraining details for individual models are not explicitly detailed beyond their repository names (e.g., 'pt22k-ft22k' for beit-base).
architecture_used.input_size: Variable depending on the model: 224x224 (for models like microsoft/swinv2-tiny-patch4-window16-256 and sai17/cards-top_left_swin-tiny-patch4-window7-224), 256x256 (for sanali209/nsfwfilter), and 384x384 (for UnipaPolitoUnimore/vit-large-patch32-384-melanoma). Images were resized accordingly during preprocessing.

architecture_details.patch_size: Varies by model (e.g., patch32 for vit-large, patch4 for swin-tiny, patch16 for beit-base).

hyperparameters.epochs: 100 epochs for most models; 20 epochs for microsoft/beit-base-patch16-224-pt22k-ft22k, sai17/cards_bottom_left_swin-tiny-patch4-window7-224-finetuned-dough_100_epochs, microsoft/beit-base-patch16-224-pt22k-ft22k, google/vit-base-patch16-224, microsoft/dit-base-finetuned-rvlcdip.
hyperparameters.batch_size: 16 for binary cascade models; not explicitly specified for multi-class models in the summary table but implied as standard training.

classification_or_output_head.multi_class: 8 classes (Melanoma, Nevus, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Actinic Keratosis, Seborrheic Keratosis, Dermatofibroma, Vascular Lesions, Squamous Cell Carcinoma).

preprocessing_techniques: Resizing images to model input dimensions (e.g., 224x224 for models like microsoft/swinv2-tiny-patch4-window16-256 and sai17/cards-top_left_swin-tiny-patch4-window7-224; 256x256 for sanali209/nsfwfilter; 384x384 for UnipaPolitoUnimore/vit-large-patch32-384-melanoma)
Normalization of images based on model requirements
conclusions: The developed screening method using mobile dermatoscopy and machine learning allows for the early differential diagnosis of malignant skin neoplasms.
Multi-class classification is recommended for the primary division of a large volume of dermatoscopic images by nosological sign among medical specialists during mass preventive examinations.
Cascade binary classification is recommended for use in primary care settings with limited access to specialized specialists to differentiate melanoma from other skin neoplasms.
The multi-class classification model achieved an Accuracy of 0.932 and an F-measure of 0.891 for eight types of neoplasms.
The first stage of binary cascade classification (melanocytic vs. non-melanocytic neoplasms) achieved an Accuracy of 0.954 and an F-measure of 0.948.
The second stage of binary cascade classification (melanoma vs. nevus) achieved an Accuracy of 0.964 and an F-measure of 0.951.

The two-stage binary classification strategy provides higher accuracy (an increase of 2-3% on average) compared to multi-class classification, particularly for melanoma recognition, by decomposing the complex task into narrower sub-tasks.

Multi-class classification is faster and more suitable for scenarios requiring high decision speed, such as mass screening and limited computational resources, whereas the two-stage approach requires more processing time due to sequential model operation.
The developed screening examination method can be introduced into medical practice as a system for supporting physician decision-making.
important_remarks: The custom dataset (657 images) is biased towards the Russian population (Fitzpatrick skin types I–IV), limiting generalizability to other ethnicities.
The study lacks external validation on independent, geographically diverse datasets, relying solely on ISIC-2019 and the authors' internal collection.
No statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed; results are reported only as point estimates (Accuracy and F-measure).
Data leakage risk exists as the custom dataset was combined with ISIC-2019 for both training and testing without explicit mention of strict patient-level or image-level separation protocols.
The study evaluated 17 models from open repositories (Hugging Face) without detailed reporting of hyperparameter tuning or optimization strategies beyond epoch counts.
Squamous Cell Carcinoma is severely underrepresented in the dataset (77 images in ISIC-2019, 15 in custom set), potentially compromising model performance for this class.

The cascade binary classification approach, while more accurate, requires sequential processing time, which may be a deployment concern for high-throughput mass screening scenarios compared to single-stage multi-class classification.
